attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring or one-time remote agent ("run this every Monday", "open a cleanup PR for X in 2 weeks"), or to manage existing routines. ALSO OFFER PROACTIVELY: after you finish work that has a natural future follow-up, end your reply with a one-line offer to schedule a background agent to do it. Strong signals: a feature flag / gate / experiment / staged rollout was just shipped (offer a one-time agent in ~2 weeks to open a cleanup PR or evaluate results), a new alert/monitor was created (offer a recurring agent to triage it), a TODO/migration with a "remove once X" condition was left behind (offer a one-time agent to do the removal). Skip the offer for refactors, bug fixes, and anything that is done once it ships. Name a concrete action and cadence ("in 2 weeks", "every Monday") and only offer when the run just succeeded — do not pitch a schedule for something that has not happened yet.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.command: jj log -R . --limit 15 --no-graph -T 'change_id.short() ++ " " ++ description.first_line() ++ "\n"' 2>&1; echo "---"; jj st -R . 2>&1; echo "---"; jj st -R .claude 2>&1

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

A bulleted list of the in-progress task's development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah

- 0.42.0-0 plan + version bump + new chores-07.md (done) [72]

- 0.42.0-6 clone --scope [72]
- 0.42.0-7 Single(_) dogfood validation [72]
- 0.42.0 close-out [72]

A markdown list of tasks to do in the near future, ordered
highest-priority first. Keep entries brief — 1-3 lines.
Detailed motivation, safety requirements, and ordering belong
in `notes/chores-NN.md` design subsections; link via `[N]` ref.

Items use lazy numbering — every entry begins with `1. `; the
markdown renderer auto-numbers them, so reorder/insert without
renumbering. Reference by displayed number ("let's work on #3").
1. Add a vc-x1 validate-repo?
1. vc-x1 push: `--scope=code|bot|code,bot|<path>` flag.
   Lands in the 0.42.0 cycle alongside the enum
   refactor; state machine becomes scope-aware (single-
   side path skips `commit-claude`/bookmark-claude/
   `finalize-claude`; `Single(_)` is single-repo mode).
   [57],[60],[71],[72]
1. vc-x1 clone: `--scope=code|bot|code,bot|<path>` flag.
   Parallel to `init --scope`; single-repo clone target
   via the path form. 0.42.0 cycle. [60],[71],[72]
1. vc-x1 validate-desc / fix-desc:
   `--scope=code|bot|code,bot` flag. Same role vocabulary
   as elsewhere — `code` validates code's commits against
   bot, `bot` reverses, `code,bot` does both (new
   default). `Single(_)` errors here (validate compares
   two repos by definition). 0.42.0 cycle. [60],[71],[72]
1. CommonArgs sweep — add `--scope=code|bot|code,bot|<path>`
   to `chid`/`desc`/`list`/`show` in one cycle (single
   shared `CommonArgs` change picks all four up). Drops
   the existing `-R`/`--repo` repeatable flag in favor of
   the new path form. 0.42.0 cycle. [60],[71],[72]
1. Help layout: force over-under everywhere. Apply
   `next_line_help(true)` at the root (or via the existing
   `cli_with_banner` walker) so every subcommand's `-h` /
   `--help` uses the same layout. Today clap auto-picks
   per-command based on the widest flag spec, so
   `sync -h` is two-column but `init -h` is over-under —
   visual inconsistency.
1. Consider renaming the `.vc-config.toml` `[workspace]`
   section. Rust readers expect `[workspace]` to mean a
   Cargo workspace, which a vc-x1 dual-repo isn't.
   Candidates: `[repo-list]`, `[project]`, `[dual-repo]`.
   Breaking change — needs migration story (read both
   names during a transition cycle, or one-shot rewrite
   in `vc-x1 sync`/`init` on first contact). Drives the
   broader "stop saying workspace in user-facing surfaces"
   sweep.
1. Add `status` (alias `st`) subcommand: `jj st` across both
   repos in one shot. Uses `--scope` from day one. This is
   natural home for the working-copy signal called out and
   it needs to include remotes, like remotes/origin/main. [54].
1. `vc-x1 init --dry-run` should bypass the

1. vc-x1 push: `--squash` flag. Squashes WC into `@-` via
   `--ignore-immutable` and force-pushes; needs
   `--force-with-lease`-equivalent + state-sanity preflight in
   place first. [57]
1. vc-x1 push: `--message-file PATH` flag. Git-style commit
   message file (first line = title, blank, rest = body).
   Alternative to `--title` + `--body`. [58]
1. Mirror `--check` / `--no-check` onto `vc-x1 push` (forwards
   through to the preflight `vc-x1 sync` invocation).
   0.37.1 hard-codes `--check`; default stays `--check`.
1. Add `validate-repo` subcommand: diagnostic that runs all
   `verify_*` checks (tracking, push state freshness, ochid
   integrity, conflicts, config sanity, working-copy state)
   and reports per-check pass/fail. Exit code = number of
   failed checks. Implementation: promote
   `verify_state_sanity` / `verify_completion_sanity` from
   push.rs to `common.rs`. [69]
1. sync: surface working-copy state in the up-to-date summary
   (per-repo pending-files count or compact stat). Wording-only
   fix shipped in 0.37.1; this is the design+impl. [54]
1. bm-track silent-when-clean refinement. Print on entry/exit
   only when state isn't fully tracked or when exit state
   differs from entry. [62]
1. "Oh shit" revert — post-success undo via `.vc-x1-ops/`
   anchor dir. Idea-stage; every repo-mutating command drops a
   pre-op snapshot, `vc-x1 undo` restores both repos. [57]
1. Restructure templates: replace separate `vc-template-x1` +
   `vc-template-x1.claude` repos with a single `vc-template-x1`
   that has `.claude/` as a subdir (covers `LICENSE-*` etc. for
   both sides in one place). Updates to `vc-x1 init` / `clone`

1. Source-code design ref sweep + CLAUDE.md codification:
   adopt section-name + `blob/main/...` URL pattern for source
   code refs to designs; codify in CLAUDE.md alongside the
   existing markdown ref conventions. Sweep targets:
   src/push.rs lines 4, 121, 645, 1219. [68]
1. Richer bookmark enumeration: per-bookmark remote presence + tracking status [52]
1. Per-line/per-thread runtime log points (future, maybe) [36]
1. Add Windows symlink support via `std::os::windows::fs::symlink_dir` [37]
1. Add "::" revision syntax for jj compatibility
1. Add -p, --parents, -c, --children so parent and child counts can be asymmetric
1. Add integration tests in tests/ for subcommands using temp jj repos (tempfile crate)
1. Fix .claude repo history: dev0 through dev2 sessions squashed into wrong commit [4],[5]
1. Add `vc-x1 setup` subcommand: completions install, .claude repo init, symlink setup [27]
1. Add dynamic revision completion via `ArgValueCompleter` (jj doesn't complete revsets either) [28],[29]

## Done

Completed tasks are moved from `## Todo` to here, `## Done`, as they are completed
and older `## Done` sections are moved to [done.md](done.md) to keep this file small.

# init-clone-refactor: resurrection notes

Side notes captured 2026-05-01 for the eventual cleanup
cycle. Not a plan — a brief so the next session can pick
up without re-reading the conversation that produced it.

## Status as of 2026-05-01

- Branch `init-clone-refactor` exists locally, 8 commits of
  in-progress refactor work all in conflict state:
  `0.41.1-0` (chore: open) → unnamed → `0.41.1-5` (init
  reshape + args module) → `0.41.1-6.0..6.5` (POR
  baseline tests, then progressive refactor split of
  `create_local_repo`).
- Branched off `0.41.0` at commit `6747a27`. Has **never**
  been rebased onto post-0.42.0 main.
- `@origin (ahead by at least 10 commits, behind by 8
  commits)` — divergence is from local jj rewrites (each
  rewrite assigns a new cid even with stable chid); origin
  still holds older cids. Force-with-lease push expected
  when the cleaned branch eventually pushes.

## Why this matters (intent)

Refactor `init` and `clone` to share `init_one` /
`clone_one` primitives plus orchestrators (`init_dual` /
`clone_dual`). Eliminates duplicated lifecycle logic
between init and clone, and gives a single chokepoint for
`--scope`-related changes — important because every
subcommand picking up `--scope` lands on the same `Scope`
shape, and reusable foundations mean those changes happen
in one place rather than parallel implementations. Design
captured in `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign
(0.41.1)`.

## Strategic decision (deferred)

Keep `main` + the `0.42.0-X` series as-is. Resurrect
`init-clone-refactor` on its own branch, finish the
refactor, close it out as `0.41.1`. **Then** decide the
merge direction:

- rebase `0.41.1` on top of `0.42.0` as a `0.43.0` (or
  `0.42.1`), or
- vice versa (force-rewrite main — only if there's a
  reason that justifies rewriting published history),
- or some other topology.

Decision deferred until the cleaned-up branch is visible.
Forcing the call now means committing to a topology
before seeing what the finished refactor looks like.

## Diagnostic preamble (do this BEFORE assuming scope)

The 8 conflicts are **not** caused by 0.42.0-1/2/3
landing on main — this branch hasn't been rebased onto
post-0.42.0 main. Conflicts are from earlier internal
rewrites on the branch itself (probably an interrupted
rebase or a substep-style rewrite that propagated
divergence through descendants).

First moves, all read-only:

```
jj new -r init-clone-refactor -R .   # WC onto a fresh @ on top of branch
jj resolve --list -R .               # what's actually in conflict
```

Or per-commit:
```
jj resolve --list -r <chid> -R .
```

Output narrows the cleanup scope. May turn out to be
much smaller than "8 commits × N files".

## JJ-CONFLICT-README (the thing that shows in gitk)

When jj has a conflicted commit, it serializes the
conflict state into a git-side tree containing:

- `JJ-CONFLICT-README` — text file explaining the format.
- `.jjconflict-base-N/` — the base side(s) of the
  three-way merge.
- `.jjconflict-side-N/` — each conflict input.

Plain git tools (gitk, GitHub UI, etc.) display this as
real files. **Recoverable** — `jj` itself knows the
commit is conflicted and `jj resolve` walks the inputs.
Don't `git checkout` a conflicted commit and start
editing files directly; jj's invariants get confused.
The README itself includes the recovery hint
(`jj abandon` if you accidentally end up in this state).

## Concrete cleanup sequence (sketch)

Once diagnosis is done:

1. Resolve conflicts where possible — `jj resolve` per
   conflicted file, or `jj abandon`/`jj squash` away
   conflicts that turned out to be artifacts of
   incomplete rebases.
2. Consider squashing the `-6.0..-6.5` substep ladder
   into one or two commits before any rebase. The
   formal substep protocol (`notes/substep-protocol.md`)
   spells out the close-out squash recipe; that ladder
   was substep-style work that never got squashed.
3. Continue the planned `0.41.1` work — finish the cycle
   per `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign
   (0.41.1) > ### Cycle structure — multi-step` (the
   plan's `-1`..`-5` and final close-out steps).
4. Close out as `0.41.1`. Push to a feature-branch
   remote (NOT main) until merge direction is decided.

## Open questions (to answer when cleanup is closer)

- **Version label for the cleanup work itself.** Continue
  the `-6.x` line, jump to `-7`, or restart at `-1`?
  Depends on what the post-diagnosis state looks like.
- **Merge direction.** Rebase `0.41.1` onto `0.42.0` →
  `0.43.0`-or-`0.42.1`, vs force-rewrite main. The first
  is the safe default; the second only if there's a
  positive reason to relitigate published history.
- **Conflict surface against 0.42.0.** Once
  `init-clone-refactor` is cleaned, what's the actual
  diff vs current `main`? `0.42.0-1/2/3` rewrote
  `init.rs` / `scope.rs` / `common.rs`; `0.41.1-5/-6.x`
  also touched those. Real conflicts will surface only
  at the rebase step.

## References

- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign (0.41.1)`
  — full design captured in 0.42.0-4 (docs-only commit
  on main).
- `notes/substep-protocol.md` — close-out squash recipe
  applies to the `-6.x` substep ladder.
- `notes/jj-revsets.md` — revset primitives for
  inspecting the conflicted ladder.

**Cycle**: `0.42.0` multi-step `--scope` enum refactor. `Cargo.toml` is at `0.42.0-4.5` (the substep-protocol docs commit at `c2e5135`).

**Ladder progress** (`notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`):
- done: `-0` plan, `-1` `Scope` enum, `-2` `init --scope`, `-3` `sync --scope` drop `-R`, `-4.5` substep protocol + jj revsets docs
- current: **`-4` push --scope** (state machine becomes scope-aware)
- queued: `-5` finalize --scope, `-6` clone --scope, `-7` Single dogfood, close-out

**Working copy** (uncommitted):
- App: `notes/init-clone-refactor-conflict.md` (new) — 2026-05-01 brief about a separate stalled `init-clone-refactor` branch (8 conflicted commits, branched from 0.41.0, never rebased onto 0.42.0). Strategic decision is to defer; resurrect later as `0.41.1`, then decide merge direction.
- `.claude`: routine session writes.

content.text:
Diagnosed. The topology is clearer than the brief suggested:

```
ykxrlkkwqpyv (0.41.0) ── common ancestor
├── ozvrpkrxuotx (0.42.0-0) ─── ... ── main (0.42.0-4.5)   ← clean
└── pmunlntsnklv (0.41.1-0)                                  ← clean
    └── uuzwzxzkszwq (unnamed, no description)               ← 5 conflicts already
        └── otkprmrnyoqm (0.41.1-5)                          ← +clone.rs joins conflict
            └── smvxvzypxrtq..sumruqqomnzs (-6.0..-6.5)      ← all conflicted
```

**Brief is partly wrong.** It speculates the conflicts come from "rebases or substep rewrites." Reality is sharper: the conflict chain starts *inside* the branch itself, at the unnamed `uuzwzxzkszwq` commit between `0.41.1-0` (clean) and `0.41.1-5`. That commit has no description and already has 5 conflicts. Everything below it just inherits.

**Conflict surface** (same files all the way down):
- `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`, `notes/todo.md` — "shared cycle scaffolding" files
- `notes/chores-08.md` — 3-sided conflict (suspicious: main doesn't have chores-08.md)
- `src/config.rs` (with deletion), `src/init.rs`, `src/clone.rs`

The 3-sided `chores-08.md` is a strong tell — it usually means a commit was rebased back onto a parent that itself was a rewrite of one of its sides, doubling a contribution. Likely an interrupted/abandoned rebase left orphan parents in the conflict inputs.

## Strategic options

1. **Diagnose `uuzwzxzkszwq` first, then decide.** Look at its diff and `jj op log` around when it appeared. If it's an empty/artifact commit, abandoning it may dissolve most conflicts in one move. Cheapest first step. Recommend this.

2. **Resolve top-down with `jj resolve`.** Walk the 8 commits, fix per-file. Mechanical but tedious; the same surface keeps reappearing because conflicts propagate through descendants. Don't do this without (1).

3. **Replay clean from `0.41.1-0`.** Reset to the last clean commit, re-apply `-5` and `-6.x` work as fresh commits using the conflicted versions as guidance. Brutal but produces a clean history; loses the substep ladder's individual review value but the ladder was going to be squashed at close-out anyway per substep-protocol.

4. **Squash the `-6.x` ladder first, then resolve once.** Substep-protocol close-out collapses `-6.0..-6.5` into a single commit anyway. But `jj squash` on conflicted commits propagates the conflict — has to come *after* resolution, not before. So this is a structural cleanup, not a conflict-recovery move.

**My recommendation:** start with (1) — investigate `uuzwzxzkszwq`. Two specific reads I'd do:
- `jj show uuzwzxzkszwq -R .` to see what (if anything) it changes
- `jj op log -R . --limit 50` near the timeframe the branch was active, to find the rewrite that likely created it

If `uuzwzxzkszwq` turns out to be an empty artifact, abandoning it could collapse the whole chain. If it's real work, we'll know what we're actually dealing with before committing to (2) or (3).

Want me to run those two diagnostics?

content.text:
Strong move, and I think you're probably right. A few thoughts:

**The setup matches the hypothesis well.** The `jj bookmark list` output earlier showed:
```
init-clone-refactor: sumruqqo ae61955c (conflict) ...
  @origin (ahead by at least 10, behind by 8): sumruqqo/4 a8c1eefe (hidden) ...
```
Same change_id `sumruqqomnzs` on both sides, different commit_ids — that's the jj signature of "local rewrote what the remote still holds." If the remote-side commit is unconflicted on GitHub (gitk view), then the *local rewrites are what introduced the conflicts*. Discarding them and adopting the remote as canonical is exactly the right shape.

(Minor: I think you meant `remotes/origin/init-clone-refactor` — there's no `init-done-refactor` bookmark in the local list. Worth eyeballing the GitHub URL one more time before relying on it.)

**The mechanics in jj are clean.** Equivalent of `git reset --hard origin/<branch>`:
```
jj git fetch -R .                                                    # make sure tracking is current
jj bookmark set init-clone-refactor -r init-clone-refactor@origin -R .   # may need --allow-backwards
```
The local rewrite commits become hidden (not destroyed — `jj op log` + `jj op restore` recovers if we're wrong). No force-push needed afterwards because local would match remote.

**Risks worth flagging — verify before pulling the trigger:**

1. **"Ahead by at least 10."** Local has ~10 commits the remote doesn't. If those are all just rewrites of the same 8 published chids, fine — that's the conflict noise we want to drop. But if any of them are *new work* that never made it to the remote, reset throws it away. Cheap to check: `jj log -R . -r 'init-clone-refactor@origin..init-clone-refactor'` — if every entry shares a chid with something on the remote side, it's all rewrites and safe to drop.

2. **Confirm the remote tip is actually clean in jj's view, not just gitk's.** gitk doesn't know about jj conflicts in some cases (it'd render `JJ-CONFLICT-README` files as if they were real). After a fresh fetch:
   ```
   jj log -R . -r 'trunk()..init-clone-refactor@origin' -T '... if(conflict, "CONFLICT", "ok") ...'
   ```
   If everything reads `ok`, the remote really is the canonical-good version.

3. **The brief's claim "@origin behind by 8" had a different theory** (it speculated divergence came from local rewrites assigning new cids — same conclusion as you, expressed differently). Both readings agree: remote is older-and-clean, local is newer-and-broken. Reset to remote is consistent with both.

**If the verification holds, I'd take this over my option (1).** It collapses the whole 8-commit cleanup into a single `jj bookmark set` plus a sanity check. The diagnostic I was going to run on `uuzwzxzkszwq` becomes irrelevant — we'd just walk away from it.

**One downstream thing it doesn't change:** the strategic decision in the brief about *merge direction* (rebase 0.41.1 onto 0.42.0 → 0.43.0, vs. force-rewrite main) is independent. The reset just gives us a clean branch to make that decision *from*.

Want me to run the two verifications (`jj git fetch` + the two log queries) — read-only, no bookmark changes — so we can decide with the actual remote-tip state in hand?

content.text:
Both are sensible — being nervous is fine. Quick thoughts on each:

**(1) `cp -a . ../vc-x1-20260502-1` — solid plan, three things to know:**

- **`.claude` is a symlink.** `cp -a` preserves symlinks as symlinks (won't follow them), so the backup's `.claude` will point at the same target as the original. That's actually what you want — the recovery work is in the app repo, `.claude` isn't at risk. Just don't expect the backup to be self-contained for the bot session repo.
- **Includes `target/`** — typical Rust build dir, multi-GB. If disk space matters, `rsync -a --exclude=target/ ./ ../vc-x1-20260502-1/` does the same job leaner. If space is fine, `cp -a` is simpler.
- **Includes `.jj/op` log.** Big deal — the backup carries the full jj operation history, so even `jj op restore` capability survives in the snapshot. That's the actual safety net.

**(2) Fresh `git clone` of the github vc-x1 — yes, do this.**

This is the higher-value backup of the two. It gives you an *unambiguous* answer to "what does the published repo contain?" without any local jj rewrites in the picture. You can `gitk` it directly and see the canonical history that we're proposing to reset to. If the reset goes wrong, you can re-derive from this clone.

Plain `git clone` (not `jj git clone`) is right here — no jj overlay, no working-copy state, just the published git truth. Pure inspection target.

**One small addition I'd suggest:** before either backup, dump the jj op log to a text file —

```
jj op log -R . > ../vc-x1-20260502-1.oplog.txt   # or wherever
```

The op log is *the* recovery mechanism for jj. Capturing it as plain text now means: even if both backups got somehow corrupted, you'd still have a human-readable record of "the working state was at op X" to point `jj op restore` at later. Two seconds of insurance.

**Order I'd run them in:**
1. Op log dump (instant)
2. Fresh `git clone` (network-bound, runs while (3) starts)
3. `cp -a` / `rsync` snapshot (CPU/disk-bound)

(2) and (3) can run in parallel — they touch different paths.

**One sanity check before any of this:** confirm `../vc-x1-20260502-1` and `../vc-x1-repo-clone-20260502-1` don't already exist, so neither command silently merges into a pre-existing dir.

Ready when you are. Want me to run the three commands (op log dump + clone + cp), or do you want to type them yourself?

message.content: Explain (2.) for instance "trunk()" what trunk from where? `trunk()..init-clone-refactor@origin`, I believe "trunk()" some ancestor of "init-clone-refactor@origin" and because ".." is used we won't see it? (Probably totally wrong, but my guess)

content.text:
Good guess — you got the structure right, just one nuance. Here's the breakdown:

**`trunk()`** — jj built-in revset function. Resolves to the tip of the main branch on the upstream remote. In this repo that's `main@origin` (configurable via `revset-aliases.'trunk()'` in jj config, but we haven't customized it). Worth using over hardcoding `main@origin` because if the convention ever changes, all revsets that say `trunk()` adjust automatically.

**`A..B`** — "reachable from B but NOT reachable from A." Excludes A itself and all of A's ancestors. Your intuition was right that we won't see trunk's commits — that's exactly what `..` does, just to be precise: it excludes ancestors of the **left** side.

So **`trunk()..init-clone-refactor@origin`** reads as: "commits on `init-clone-refactor@origin` that aren't already on `main@origin`" — i.e., the branch-only commits, the 8-9 we care about. Without `trunk()..`, we'd get all of history back to the root commit, which is noise.

(Side note: jj's `A..B` matches git's `A..B` semantically. But jj's `A::B` is "all commits between A and B inclusive on both ends" — different from git's `A...B` which is symmetric difference. The cheatsheet at `notes/jj-revsets.md` covers the full set.)

So the actual query for step 2 is:

```
jj log -R . -r 'trunk()..init-clone-refactor@origin' -T 'change_id.short() ++ " " ++ description.first_line() ++ " [" ++ if(conflict, "CONFLICT", "ok") ++ "]\n"' --no-graph
```

Each line will say `[ok]` if the commit is unconflicted, `[CONFLICT]` if not. If everything reads `ok`, the published branch really is the canonical-good version we want to reset to.

Run it?

message.content: Why not use :: so we can see that commit too?

content.text:
Good instinct, but `::` doesn't quite work here, and the reason is structural:

**`A::B` is a DAG range** — it returns commits that are descendants of A AND ancestors of B. It requires A to actually be an ancestor of B (or vice versa) — otherwise the set is empty.

In our case:
```
ykxrlkkwqpyv (0.41.0)        ← common ancestor
├── 0.42.0-N ladder → main   ← trunk()
└── 0.41.1 ladder → init-clone-refactor@origin
```

`trunk()` and `init-clone-refactor@origin` are **siblings**, not in an ancestor relationship — they both forked off `0.41.0`. So `trunk()::init-clone-refactor@origin` would return empty.

**What you probably want is "the branch commits + the fork point."** Two ways to express that:

1. **Use the known fork commit directly** — `0.41.0` is `ykxrlkkwqpyv`:
   ```
   ykxrlkkwqpyv::init-clone-refactor@origin
   ```
   Works because `ykxrlkkwqpyv` IS an ancestor of the branch tip.

2. **Compute the merge-base inside the revset:**
   ```
   trunk()..init-clone-refactor@origin | (init-clone-refactor@origin & ::trunk())
   ```
   Left side: branch-only commits. Right side: greatest common ancestor (i.e., the fork point).

(1) is simpler if we already know the chid; (2) is more general if we don't. Both produce the same set here.

Want me to switch step 2 to (1)?

toolUseResult.stdout: ykxrlkkwqpyvkxswqzqtnsokmllsyown | 6747a27b808e2efb8178bdaaa738a970a1e56843 | docs: --scope cycle close-out (0.41.0)

content.text:
Commit_id is `6747a27b…` — so the brief was right, and `6767a27b` is a one-character misread on the gitk side (4 vs 6 in the third hex digit). Same commit either way: **"docs: --scope cycle close-out (0.41.0)"**.

So fork point confirmed:
- chid: `ykxrlkkwqpyv`
- cid: `6747a27b`
- description: `docs: --scope cycle close-out (0.41.0)`

In jj you can also derive this without scanning parent columns by hand — the revset for a fork point is:

```
heads(::A & ::B)
```

i.e., "the latest commit that is an ancestor of both A and B." For our case:

```
jj log -R . -r 'heads(::main & ::init-clone-refactor@origin)'
```

That'd return `ykxrlkkwqpyv` directly. Useful when you don't already have the topology in hand.

Good catch — and your counting is probably correct. That changes the picture meaningfully.

Looking back at the bookmark output:
```
@origin (ahead by at least 10 commits, behind by 8 commits)
```

I'd glossed past the "at least 10" — that's jj telling us "the remote has *at least 10* commits the local doesn't, and the local has 8 the remote doesn't." Your gitk count of 12 remote vs 9 local is consistent if `0.41.1-0` (`ee7d49dd`) is shared on both sides — that gives:

- Local: 1 shared (clean `0.41.1-0`) + 8 unique conflicted = 9
- Remote: 1 shared (clean `0.41.1-0`) + 11 unique = 12
- "ahead by at least 10" ≈ 11, "behind by 8" ✓

**Implication for the reset:** It's not just "drop noisy local rewrites and accept the same content." It's "drop local *and* accept 3 additional remote commits we never had locally." Those 3 extras might be:

- Continuation past `-6.5` (`-6.6`, `-7`, close-out attempts)
- Substeps that got squashed locally but remain published
- A close-out attempt that landed on the remote but never made it back here

Either way: **we need to know what those 3 extras are before resetting**, because the reset will pull them in.

The step-2 query handles this — it'll list every commit on `init-clone-refactor@origin` after the fork, with conflict status. You'll see all 12, and we can check both:
1. Are all 12 unconflicted? (the original question)
2. What are the 3 we don't have locally?

Same query as before — still safe to run, just yields more info than I expected:

content.text:
Excellent news — and a bigger story than expected. **All 13 lines `[ok]`**: every commit from the fork point through `init-clone-refactor@origin`'s tip is unconflicted on the remote.

But look at the descriptions — this is more than just "remote has older clean versions of the same commits":

| Local has | Remote has | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| (none) | `mkwqykksykoo` 0.41.1-**1** "shared repo_url module" | **missing locally** |
| (none) | `plxsuynrtwks` 0.41.1-**2** "clone reshape + shared symlink::install" | **missing locally** |
| (none) | `xtrworrlkozn` 0.41.1-**3** "user config module — ~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml" | **missing locally** |
| `uuzwzxzkszwq` (no description, conflicted) | `uuzwzxzkszwq` 0.41.1-**4** "user config rewrite — multi-account schema" | **same chid, lost description+work** |
| `otkprmrnyoqm` 0.41.1-5 (conflicted) | `otkprmrnyoqm` 0.41.1-5 (clean) | same chid, conflicted vs clean |
| `smvxvzypxrtq..sumruqqomnzs` 0.41.1-6.0..6.5 (all conflicted) | `smvxvzypxrtq..sumruqqomnzs` 0.41.1-6.0..6.5 (all clean) | same chids, conflicted vs clean |

**What appears to have happened locally:** a `jj squash` (or similar rewrite) collapsed `-1`/`-2`/`-3` into `-4`, and in doing so:
- Stripped `-4`'s description (now blank `uuzwzxzkszwq`)
- Lost the `-1`/`-2`/`-3` commits entirely
- Cascaded conflicts down through every descendant (`-5`, `-6.0..6.5`) because the parent's tree no longer matched what they were rebased against

So the local state isn't just "rewrites that introduced conflicts" — it's a **destructive squash that destroyed real work**. The remote preserves it.

**Strong implication for the reset:** this is now even more clearly the right move. The reset will:
1. Drop 8 broken local commits (all the conflicted ones)
2. **Recover 3 commits of work that were lost** (`-1`, `-2`, `-3`)
3. Restore `-4`'s real description and tree
4. End at the same `0.41.1-6.5` chid as today, but as the clean published version

The fact that chids `uuzwzxzkszwq`, `otkprmrnyoqm`, `smvxvzypxrtq..sumruqqomnzs` match across local and remote means jj will recognize them as the same logical changes — the reset is "accept the remote's better version of these chids" plus "pull in the 3 missing chids."

**Step 3** (the third verification I proposed earlier) — listing what's on local but not on remote — is now mostly confirmation. We expect it to show local rewrites of the chids the remote also has (different cids), no genuine new content. Want me to run it for completeness, or are you ready to skip ahead?

**Every local-only chid has a corresponding chid on the remote.** Zero unique local content. The 8 commits we'd be dropping are all 1:1 broken-rewrite versions of commits already published in clean form. Nothing to lose.

The reset is now fully de-risked:
- ✅ Remote tip is unconflicted (verified all 13 commits `[ok]`)
- ✅ All 8 local-only commits are just rewrites — no genuine new content
- ✅ 3 commits will come back from the dead (`-1`, `-2`, `-3`)
- ✅ `-4`'s real description and tree restored
- ✅ Backups in place if anything surprises us

**Proposed next step:** the reset itself.

```
jj bookmark set init-clone-refactor -r init-clone-refactor@origin -R .
```

This moves the local `init-clone-refactor` bookmark from its current cid (`ae61955c`, conflicted) to the remote-tracking cid (`a8c1eefe`, clean). Local takes the "behind" cids and abandons (hides) the "ahead" cids.

May need `--allow-backwards` since it's a backwards move. We'll see — jj will tell us.

After the reset, a quick verify with the same step-2 query will confirm we're sitting on the clean published state.

Run the reset?

Recovery successful. **All 13 commits `[ok]`**:
- The 3 missing commits are back: `0.41.1-1`, `-2`, `-3`
- `0.41.1-4` (`uuzwzxzkszwq`) has its real description restored: "user config rewrite — multi-account schema"
- The `-5` / `-6.0..6.5` ladder is clean

A few notes on the current state:

**`(divergent)` flag.** The bookmark line shows `sumruqqo/4 a8c1eefe (divergent)`. This is jj's way of saying "there are multiple visible commits with the same chid `sumruqqomnzs`." The bookmark now points at the clean published version (`a8c1eefe`), but the conflicted local rewrite (`ae61955c`) is still visible in `jj log` as an orphan — not on any bookmark, just floating. jj will GC those eventually, or we can abandon them explicitly to tidy the workspace view.

**Working copy untouched.** `@` is still where it was at session start — on top of `main`, holding `notes/init-clone-refactor-conflict.md`. The brief file is safe.

**No push needed.** The bookmark now exactly matches `@origin` — there's nothing to publish.

**Possible next steps** (one at a time, your call):

1. **Tidy up the orphan rewrites** — `jj abandon` the 8 conflicted local-only commits so `jj log` stops showing them. Pure cosmetics.
2. **Sanity test** — checkout the new tip (`jj new init-clone-refactor` or similar), `cargo test --bins` to confirm the recovered branch actually builds and tests pass.
3. **Update the brief** — `notes/init-clone-refactor-conflict.md` is now partly stale (the recovery worked; some hypotheses were wrong). Either update it in place to reflect what actually happened, or delete it (the recovery is no longer needed as future-context).
4. **Decide merge direction** — the strategic question the brief flagged: rebase `0.41.1` onto `0.42.0` → `0.43.0`, or some other topology.

What's next?

content.text:
Found it. Key facts:

- **chid**: `wmmxkuozvltkqnpsvzmspxrsvvzoqzuk` (one we haven't seen before)
- **cid**: `6e60cfb3...`
- **parent**: `a8c1eefe` — that's the *new* `init-clone-refactor` tip (the clean `0.41.1-6.5`)
- **no bookmarks, no git refs, no description**

So it's a true orphan: child of the clean tip, on no branch in git, hidden by jj's default view filter (which is why `init-clone-refactor::` didn't show it — that revset uses jj's "visible" filter and skips obsolete/abandoned commits). It only surfaces if you ask for it by id.

**The bot's guess:** this commit predates the reset. Someone — probably you in an earlier session — had local `init-clone-refactor` sitting at the clean `a8c1eefe` cid at one point, started a new working copy on top of it to draft the substep protocol doc, and that work eventually landed on `main` instead (as `c2e51353 docs: substep protocol + jj revsets (0.42.0-4.5)`). The intermediate WC snapshot got orphaned.

Let me verify by diffing the orphan against `main`'s substep-protocol commit — if they match, hypothesis confirmed:

lastPrompt: We have a non-empty commit above init-clone-refactor with notes/substep-protocol.md, it's cid from gitk is 6e60cfb3f57fdd4cb4cf1bf6eccdca05d5181da4. I was expecting this to show it but nope: wink@3900…

content.text:
Hypothesis confirmed. The orphan is a **first-draft snapshot** that got superseded:

| | Orphan (`6e60cfb3`) | Main's `c2e51353` |
|---|---|---|
| Authored | 2026-04-30 07:48 | 2026-05-01 17:06 |
| Files | `notes/substep-protocol.md` only (63 lines) | substep-protocol.md (expanded) + jj-revsets.md + substep-test.sh + Cargo.toml |
| Description | (none) | "docs: substep protocol + jj revsets (0.42.0-4.5)" |

So you started drafting the substep protocol doc on April 30 in a working copy on top of the clean `init-clone-refactor` tip, then on May 1 reworked and expanded it (added the revsets cheatsheet + test scaffold + Cargo bump) and landed it on `main` instead. The April 30 WC snapshot got abandoned but the git object stayed in the store. **The work isn't lost — it's on main in fuller form.**

Why it didn't show in `jj log -r init-clone-refactor::`:
- jj's default revset filter hides "obsolete"/abandoned commits
- This commit has no bookmark and no descendant on a bookmark
- Only surfaces with explicit `-r <cid>` or `--all-mutations`-equivalent

Why gitk shows it:
- gitk reads everything in `.git/objects` + reflog/HEADs, no notion of jj's visibility model
- It'll show every commit object that exists, orphan or not

**Practical implications: none.** It's GC-eligible, on no branch, no work lost. If the visual clutter in gitk bothers you, `jj abandon 6e60cfb3` would mark it explicitly abandoned (though it's already effectively that), and eventually `jj util gc` (or whatever jj's GC command is) would prune it from the object store.

So back to the recovery: nothing here changes the outcome. `init-clone-refactor` is clean at `a8c1eefe`, the orphan is unrelated drafting noise.

Want to proceed to the next-step menu (tidy / sanity test / update brief / merge direction)?

message.content: One think before, how do I get a chid when I know the cid for a commit
One thing before that, how do I get a chid when I know the cid for a commit

content.text:
Just `jj log -r <cid_or_prefix>`. jj's default template shows the chid as the first thing on the line:

```
$ jj log -R . -r 6e60cfb3 --no-graph
○  wmmxkuoz wink@saville.com 2026-04-30 07:48:11 6e60cfb3
   (no description set)
```

That `wmmxkuoz` is the chid (short form). cid is the trailing `6e60cfb3`.

For a clean script-friendly answer, use a template:

```
jj log -R . -r 6e60cfb3 --no-graph -T 'change_id ++ "\n"'
# → wmmxkuozvltkqnpsvzmspxrsvvzoqzuk

jj log -R . -r 6e60cfb3 --no-graph -T 'change_id.short() ++ "\n"'
# → wmmxkuoz
```

Two gotchas:

1. **The cid prefix must be unambiguous.** jj will error if your prefix matches multiple commits. If `6e60` matched two different commits, you'd need more digits.

2. **Default revset filter still applies.** If the cid points at a hidden/abandoned commit (like our orphan), `jj log -r <cid>` works fine because explicit revs bypass the default filter — but you can't reach it via `mine()` or other set-based revsets. The reverse direction (cid → chid) always works as long as the commit exists in the store.

Worth noting that this also goes the other way — `jj log -r <chid>` shows the cid. The two are symmetric lookups.

Want to proceed with the post-recovery menu now?

content: Goal was to recover the conflicted local `init-clone-refactor` branch by resetting it to the clean published `init-clone-refactor@origin`. Reset is done and verified clean. Next action: pick from the post-recovery menu (tidy orphans, sanity test, update brief, decide merge direction). (disable recaps in /config)

content.text:
Recap of where we are:

- `init-clone-refactor` bookmark is now at clean `a8c1eefe` (`0.41.1-6.5`)
- All 13 commits from fork to tip are unconflicted; lost commits `-1`/`-2`/`-3` recovered; `-4`'s description restored
- 8 broken local-only commits are still visible in `jj log` (orphans, no bookmark)
- 1 hidden orphan `6e60cfb3` (the substep-protocol draft) — superseded, harmless
- `@` (working copy) is unchanged: on top of `main`, holding `notes/init-clone-refactor-conflict.md` uncommitted

**The four candidate next-steps, with proposed ordering and rationale:**

1. **Sanity test** — `jj new init-clone-refactor` (or equivalent), then `cargo test --bins`. Goes first because the whole recovery rests on "the published branch was clean and working." Cheap to verify; if it fails we want to know now, not after we've abandoned the local rewrites and lost the safety net (the backup is still there, so this is double belt-and-suspenders, but the local jj op log unwind is faster than restoring from backup).

2. **Update the brief** (`notes/init-clone-refactor-conflict.md`) — partly stale now. Two options:
   - **Update it** to reflect what actually happened (recovery via `jj bookmark set --allow-backwards`, what the conflict source really was: a destructive squash of `-1..-4`, what was recovered). Useful future reference: "if init-clone-refactor goes sideways again, here's the playbook."
   - **Delete it** as no-longer-needed scaffolding. The recovery is done; future sessions don't need a brief about a problem that was solved.
   
   I'd lean toward updating — the diagnostic technique (compare local vs `@origin` chids, look for missing/orphan commits) is generally useful and worth capturing even if this specific branch never breaks again. But it's your call.

3. **Tidy the orphan rewrites** — `jj abandon` the 8 conflicted local-only chids so `jj log` stops showing them. Pure cosmetics. Do whenever; safe to defer.

4. **Decide merge direction** — the strategic question the brief flagged: rebase `0.41.1` onto `0.42.0` → `0.43.0`-or-`0.42.1`, or some other topology. Doesn't have to happen this session. The recovery just put us in a position to make this decision from a clean slate; the decision itself can wait until we have time to think about scope/risk.

**Suggested order:** 1 → 2 → 3, then 4 deferred to its own session.

Sound right? Or do you want to reorder / drop / add anything?

message.content: y

1	# CLAUDE.md - Bot Instructions
2	
3	## Project Structure
4	
5	This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:
6	
7	1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): Contains the application source code.
8	2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.
9	
10	Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.
11	
12	## Repo Paths (relative from project root)
13	
14	- App repo: `.` (project root)
15	- Bot session repo: `.claude`
16	  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)
17	
18	## Working Directory
19	
20	Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
21	to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
22	discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
23	command failures downstream.
24	
25	When invoking shell commands, prefer the shortest unambiguous path —
26	usually relative to cwd (`ls notes/`, not
27	`ls /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/notes/`). Long absolute paths
28	clutter the transcript without adding information. Out-of-workspace
29	paths (`/tmp/...`, `~/.config/...`) stay absolute. Read/Edit/Write
30	tool args also stay absolute — that's a tool-boundary constraint,
31	not a stylistic choice.
32	
33	## Memory
34	
35	Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
36	(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
37	CLAUDE.md it provides no capability CLAUDE.md doesn't already cover,
38	and it loses on discoverability:
39	
40	- **CLAUDE.md** — at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
41	  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
42	  (human or bot).
43	- **Memory directory** — hidden under the user's home, tied to one
44	  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.
45	
46	Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
47	durable context in CLAUDE.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.
48	
49	## Speculation marker
50	
51	Durable text the bot writes — CLAUDE.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
52	chores sections — should stick to observations and direct descriptions
53	of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
54	enters the text, prefix it with "The bot thinks ..." so a reader can
55	tell the measured from the inferred.
56	
57	**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
58	(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
59	it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
60	in the written record.
61	
62	**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
63	marker. Prefix with "The bot thinks ..." (or a close variant like
64	"The bot's guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
65	because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
66	state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
67	reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.
68	
69	## Committing
70	
71	Use `-R` (`--repository`) at the end to target the correct repo. Use
72	relative paths to reduce noise. Putting `-R` last keeps the verb/action
73	visible at the start of the command.
74	
75	### App repo
76	```
77	jj commit -m \
78	"title" \
79	-m "body
80	
81	ochid: /.claude/<changeID>" \
82	-R .
83	```
84	
85	### Bot session repo
86	```
87	jj commit -m \
88	"title" \
89	-m "body
90	
91	ochid: /<changeID>" \
92	-R .claude
93	```
94	
95	## jj Basics
96	
97	- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
98	- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
99	- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
100	- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
101	- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
102	  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
103	- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
104	  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
105	- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
106	  session because session data updates continuously.
107	
108	## Commit Message Style
109	
110	Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with
111	a version suffix:
112	
113	```
114	<type>: <short description> (<version>)
115	```
116	
117	- **Title**: target ~50 chars, short summary of *what* changed.
118	  Include the version. Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`,
119	  `test`, `docs`, `chore`.
120	- **App-repo body**: short intro paragraph (1–3 sentences), then a
121	  terse bullet list. Each bullet corresponds one-to-one with the
122	  edits structure already documented in `notes/chores-*.md` for
123	  this step — just the file and a one-line gist (e.g.
124	  `README.md: new Overview intro`). Do *not* restate the detail
125	  that lives in chores; the commit body is a scan-able index, not
126	  a duplicate. The chores section is the source of truth.
127	- **Session-repo body**: terse intro + a few session-activity
128	  bullets. Doesn't need to mirror chores since it describes
129	  in-session work, not code changes.
130	- Examples:
131	  - `feat: add fix-ochid subcommand (0.22.0)`
132	  - `fix: fix-ochid prefix bug (0.22.1)`
133	  - `refactor: deduplicate common CLI flags (0.21.1)`
134	
135	## Pre-commit Requirements
136	
137	### User approval
138	
139	Never execute commit, squash, push, or finalize commands without the
140	user's explicit approval. Present changes for review first; only run
141	them after the user confirms. This applies to late changes too —
142	pause for review before squashing into an existing commit.
143	
144	### Review before proposing the commit block
145	
146	After finishing a unit of work, **summarize what changed and stop
147	there**. Do not pre-emptively lay out the Checkpoint-1 commit
148	commands. Wait for the user to signal review is complete before
149	proposing the commit block. Changes during review are the norm,
150	not the exception; proposing commit text too early creates noise
151	and signals that I consider the work done when it usually isn't.
152	
153	This applies per-step in a multi-step flow too — each step gets a
154	review pause before its commit block appears.
155	
156	Signals that review is complete include explicit approval ("let's
157	commit", "looks good, commit it") **and any directive to start the
158	next step** ("do step 4", "next", "go N+1"). In that case the
159	previous step must be committed first — always commit the current
160	step before starting the next; don't ask.
161	
162	### Per-file review checkpoints
163	
164	The "review before commit block" rule applies to in-progress work
165	too, at finer grain. After each file edit, STOP, summarize what
166	changed, and wait for explicit go-ahead before touching the next
167	file or running follow-on commands (cargo test/install, dogfood
168	invocations, `vc-x1 …`, etc.).
169	
170	**Exceptions** — both ride along with the change they accompany,
171	no separate review pause:
172	
173	- **Refactors where code is moving across files** — e.g.
174	  lifting helpers into a new module and updating the original
175	  file's imports in the same change. Reviewing each half in
176	  isolation isn't useful.
177	- **`Cargo.toml` version bumps** that go with a specific code
178	  change.
179	
180	**Why:** the user reviews diffs as work lands. Chaining multiple
181	file edits without pausing forces them to untangle cumulative
182	state instead of inspecting each step in isolation.
183	
184	**How to apply:**
185	
186	- "continue", "go", "do it", "lg" approves *the next unit
187	  only*, not all remaining units of a multi-file plan. A green
188	  light to proceed with a *direction* is not a green light to
189	  skip per-step checkpoints.
190	- After landing a unit: brief summary (2–5 lines) and stop. No
191	  cargo build / test / install / dogfood runs unless the user
192	  explicitly asks.
193	- In addition to (not replacement for) the
194	  Review-before-commit-block rule above.
195	
196	### Notes references
197	
198	Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.

content.text:
Back on `uxymmmyn`, brief file is in the working tree. Ready for step 2.

Before I touch the file, let me propose a structure rather than diving into edits — it's 139 lines and a chunk of it is now historical / partly wrong / fully obsolete. Three rough framings to choose from:

**A. Update in place — reframe as post-mortem + playbook.**
Keep the brief as a notes file, but pivot its purpose from "here's a problem to solve" to "here's what happened and how it was fixed." Sections to keep, replace, drop:

- `## Status as of 2026-05-01` → keep as historical "Status before recovery"; add `## Status as of 2026-05-02` showing the recovered state
- `## Why this matters (intent)` → keep largely intact (still accurate)
- `## Strategic decision (deferred)` → keep (merge direction still undecided)
- `## Diagnostic preamble` → **replace**: the brief's hypothesis (interrupted rebase / substep rewrite) was wrong. Reality: destructive squash collapsed `-1`/`-2`/`-3` into `-4`, stripped `-4`'s description, cascaded conflicts down the descendants. Document the real diagnostic that worked (compare local-only vs remote chids, look for missing chids).
- `## JJ-CONFLICT-README` → keep (general reference)
- `## Concrete cleanup sequence` → **replace** with the playbook actually used: backup, verify remote tip clean, verify all local-only chids exist on remote, `jj bookmark set --allow-backwards`, sanity test
- `## Open questions` → trim (cleanup version label answered, conflict surface answered; merge direction still open)

**B. Replace entirely with a slim post-mortem.**
Most of the original brief was scaffolding for a problem that no longer exists. Write a fresh 30–50 line note: what went wrong, what fixed it, what's still open (merge direction). Faster, less to read later, but loses the captured context about JJ-CONFLICT-README and the strategic intent.

**C. Delete it.**
Recovery is done; the brief was scaffolding. Future sessions can read the commit history if they need to know what happened.

I'd lean **A** — the diagnostic technique (compare chids local-vs-remote, hunt missing ones) is genuinely reusable, and the merge-direction decision still needs a home. But B is fine if you want lighter weight, and C is fine if you trust git log to carry the story.

Which framing do you want?

message.content: A

# init-clone-refactor: resurrection notes

Side notes captured 2026-05-01 for the eventual cleanup
cycle. Not a plan — a brief so the next session can pick
up without re-reading the conversation that produced it.

## Status as of 2026-05-01

- Branch `init-clone-refactor` exists locally, 8 commits of
  in-progress refactor work all in conflict state:
  `0.41.1-0` (chore: open) → unnamed → `0.41.1-5` (init
  reshape + args module) → `0.41.1-6.0..6.5` (POR
  baseline tests, then progressive refactor split of
  `create_local_repo`).
- Branched off `0.41.0` at commit `6747a27`. Has **never**
  been rebased onto post-0.42.0 main.
- `@origin (ahead by at least 10 commits, behind by 8
  commits)` — divergence is from local jj rewrites (each
  rewrite assigns a new cid even with stable chid); origin
  still holds older cids. Force-with-lease push expected
  when the cleaned branch eventually pushes.

## Why this matters (intent)

Refactor `init` and `clone` to share `init_one` /
`clone_one` primitives plus orchestrators (`init_dual` /
`clone_dual`). Eliminates duplicated lifecycle logic
between init and clone, and gives a single chokepoint for
`--scope`-related changes — important because every
subcommand picking up `--scope` lands on the same `Scope`
shape, and reusable foundations mean those changes happen
in one place rather than parallel implementations. Design
captured in `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign
(0.41.1)`.

## Strategic decision (deferred)

Keep `main` + the `0.42.0-X` series as-is. Resurrect
`init-clone-refactor` on its own branch, finish the
refactor, close it out as `0.41.1`. **Then** decide the
merge direction:

- rebase `0.41.1` on top of `0.42.0` as a `0.43.0` (or
  `0.42.1`), or
- vice versa (force-rewrite main — only if there's a
  reason that justifies rewriting published history),
- or some other topology.

Decision deferred until the cleaned-up branch is visible.
Forcing the call now means committing to a topology
before seeing what the finished refactor looks like.

## Diagnostic preamble (do this BEFORE assuming scope)

The 8 conflicts are **not** caused by 0.42.0-1/2/3
landing on main — this branch hasn't been rebased onto
post-0.42.0 main. Conflicts are from earlier internal
rewrites on the branch itself (probably an interrupted
rebase or a substep-style rewrite that propagated
divergence through descendants).

First moves, all read-only:

```
jj new -r init-clone-refactor -R .   # WC onto a fresh @ on top of branch
jj resolve --list -R .               # what's actually in conflict
```

Or per-commit:
```
jj resolve --list -r <chid> -R .
```

Output narrows the cleanup scope. May turn out to be
much smaller than "8 commits × N files".

## JJ-CONFLICT-README (the thing that shows in gitk)

When jj has a conflicted commit, it serializes the
conflict state into a git-side tree containing:

- `JJ-CONFLICT-README` — text file explaining the format.
- `.jjconflict-base-N/` — the base side(s) of the
  three-way merge.
- `.jjconflict-side-N/` — each conflict input.

Plain git tools (gitk, GitHub UI, etc.) display this as
real files. **Recoverable** — `jj` itself knows the
commit is conflicted and `jj resolve` walks the inputs.
Don't `git checkout` a conflicted commit and start
editing files directly; jj's invariants get confused.
The README itself includes the recovery hint
(`jj abandon` if you accidentally end up in this state).

## Concrete cleanup sequence (sketch)

Once diagnosis is done:

1. Resolve conflicts where possible — `jj resolve` per
   conflicted file, or `jj abandon`/`jj squash` away
   conflicts that turned out to be artifacts of
   incomplete rebases.
2. Consider squashing the `-6.0..-6.5` substep ladder
   into one or two commits before any rebase. The
   formal substep protocol (`notes/substep-protocol.md`)
   spells out the close-out squash recipe; that ladder
   was substep-style work that never got squashed.
3. Continue the planned `0.41.1` work — finish the cycle
   per `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign
   (0.41.1) > ### Cycle structure — multi-step` (the
   plan's `-1`..`-5` and final close-out steps).
4. Close out as `0.41.1`. Push to a feature-branch
   remote (NOT main) until merge direction is decided.

## Open questions (to answer when cleanup is closer)

- **Version label for the cleanup work itself.** Continue
  the `-6.x` line, jump to `-7`, or restart at `-1`?
  Depends on what the post-diagnosis state looks like.
- **Merge direction.** Rebase `0.41.1` onto `0.42.0` →
  `0.43.0`-or-`0.42.1`, vs force-rewrite main. The first
  is the safe default; the second only if there's a
  positive reason to relitigate published history.
- **Conflict surface against 0.42.0.** Once
  `init-clone-refactor` is cleaned, what's the actual
  diff vs current `main`? `0.42.0-1/2/3` rewrote
  `init.rs` / `scope.rs` / `common.rs`; `0.41.1-5/-6.x`
  also touched those. Real conflicts will surface only
  at the rebase step.

## References

- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign (0.41.1)`
  — full design captured in 0.42.0-4 (docs-only commit
  on main).
- `notes/substep-protocol.md` — close-out squash recipe
  applies to the `-6.x` substep ladder.
- `notes/jj-revsets.md` — revset primitives for
  inspecting the conflicted ladder.

# init-clone-refactor: recovery post-mortem + playbook

Drafted 2026-05-01 as a forward-looking brief while the
branch was still broken; rewritten 2026-05-02 after the
recovery completed. Documents what went wrong, the
playbook that fixed it, and the strategic decisions
still open.

## Status as of 2026-05-02 (post-recovery)

- `init-clone-refactor` bookmark is at `a8c1eefe`
  (chid `sumruqqomnzs`) — the published clean
  `0.41.1-6.5` commit.
- All 13 commits from fork point `ykxrlkkwqpyv`
  (`0.41.0`, cid `6747a27b`) through `0.41.1-6.5` are
  unconflicted. `cargo test --bins` passes 331/331 on
  the recovered tip.
- Recovery via `jj bookmark set init-clone-refactor
  -r init-clone-refactor@origin --allow-backwards
  -R .`. The published remote branch turned out to
  be the canonical-good version; local was holding a
  destructively-rewritten copy.
- 8 broken local-only chids still visible as orphans
  in `jj log` (no bookmark, GC-eligible). `jj abandon`
  cleanup is cosmetic, deferrable.

## Status as of 2026-05-01 (pre-recovery, historical)

- Branch existed locally with 8 commits all in
  conflict state: `0.41.1-0` (chore: open) → unnamed
  → `0.41.1-5` (init reshape) → `0.41.1-6.0..6.5`
  (POR baseline tests then progressive split of
  `create_local_repo`). Conflicts on the same files
  all the way down: `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`,
  `notes/todo.md`, `notes/chores-08.md` (3-sided),
  `src/config.rs`, `src/init.rs`, `src/clone.rs`.
- Original brief's hypothesis — "interrupted rebase
  or substep-style rewrite" — turned out to be wrong.
  See "Root cause" below.
- `@origin (ahead by at least 10 commits, behind by
  8 commits)` was what jj reported. The "ahead by
  *at least* 10" caveat (vs the precise "behind by
  8") was the tell that the remote held more commits
  than local — i.e. local had *lost* work, not just
  rewritten it. Worth noticing earlier next time.

## Why this matters (intent)

Refactor `init` and `clone` to share `init_one` /
`clone_one` primitives plus orchestrators
(`init_dual` / `clone_dual`). Eliminates duplicated
lifecycle logic between init and clone, and gives a
single chokepoint for `--scope`-related changes —
important because every subcommand picking up
`--scope` lands on the same `Scope` shape, and
reusable foundations mean those changes happen in
one place rather than parallel implementations.
Design captured in `notes/chores-07.md > ## init +
clone redesign (0.41.1)`.

## Root cause (post-mortem)

The bot thinks the local rewrites came from a
destructive `jj squash` (or equivalent operation)
that collapsed published commits `0.41.1-1`, `-2`,
`-3` into `-4`. Symptoms supporting this:

- Three chids existed on the remote (`mkwqykksykoo`
  `-1`, `plxsuynrtwks` `-2`, `xtrworrlkozn` `-3`)
  with no local counterpart at all.
- `uuzwzxzkszwq` (the local "unnamed" commit) shared
  the *same chid* as the remote `0.41.1-4` commit,
  but had its description stripped — consistent with
  a squash that pulled three commits' content into
  `-4`'s tree but lost the descriptions.
- Conflicts cascaded down `-5` and `-6.0..6.5`
  because those commits' parent tree no longer
  matched what they had been authored against — the
  rewritten `-4` diverged from what the descendants
  expected.

Net effect: 3 commits of real work disappeared from
local, `-4`'s description was lost, and every
descendant inherited a conflict against the changed
parent tree. The remote was untouched and held the
canonical-good version of all of it.

## Strategic decision (still deferred)

Keep `main` + the `0.42.0-X` series as-is. Now that
`init-clone-refactor` is recovered, we have a clean
branch from which to make the merge-direction call:

- rebase `0.41.1` on top of `0.42.0` as a `0.43.0`
  (or `0.42.1`), or
- vice versa (force-rewrite main — only if there's
  a reason that justifies rewriting published
  history),
- or some other topology.

Decision still deferred. The recovery removes the
"is the branch even salvageable" risk; the topology
question is now purely about scope and review
ergonomics, not technical feasibility.

## Recovery playbook (used 2026-05-02)

Reusable for similar "local rewrites broke a branch
that the remote holds in clean form" situations.
All read-only steps come first; mutation only after
verification.

1. **Back up first.** Cheap insurance.
   - `cp -a . ../<dir>-<date>-N` — full snapshot,
     includes `.jj/op` history. Or `rsync -a
     --exclude=target/ ./ ../<dir>/` if `target/`
     bloat matters.
   - `git clone <remote> ../<dir>-clone-<date>-N`
     — canonical published state, jj-free, browsable
     in gitk without local-rewrite confusion.
   - `jj op log -R . > ../<dir>-op-log-<date>-N.txt`
     — text-readable op log, independent of jj.

2. **Verify remote tip is unconflicted (read-only):**
   ```
   jj git fetch -R .
   jj log -R . -r '<fork-chid>::<branch>@origin' \
     -T '... if(conflict, "CONFLICT", "ok") ...'
   ```
   Every line must read `[ok]`. If any are
   `[CONFLICT]`, the remote isn't a safe target and
   this playbook doesn't apply.

3. **Verify local-only commits are all chid-rewrites
   of remote commits (read-only):**
   ```
   jj log -R . -r '<branch>@origin..<branch>' \
     -T 'change_id.short() ++ ...'
   ```
   Every chid here must also appear on the remote.
   If any chid is unique to local, the reset would
   lose work — investigate before proceeding.

4. **Reset bookmark to remote:**
   ```
   jj bookmark set <branch> -r <branch>@origin \
     --allow-backwards -R .
   ```
   The `--allow-backwards` is required because jj
   guards against accidental backwards moves.

5. **Sanity test:**
   ```
   jj new <branch> -R .
   cargo test --bins      # or full cargo clean ; cargo test for paranoia
   ```

6. **Optional cleanup:** `jj abandon` the orphan
   local-only chids if you don't want them in
   `jj log`. Cosmetic; jj's GC eventually prunes
   them anyway.

## Diagnostic technique (reusable)

Two questions to ask when a branch is mysteriously
conflicted:

- **Are the same chids on the remote in clean form?**
  `jj log -r '<fork>::<branch>@origin'` with the
  conflict-status template tells you whether the
  remote has a working version of every commit.
- **Is local missing any chids the remote has?**
  Compare chid lists between
  `<fork>::<branch>@origin` and
  `<fork>::<branch>` — gaps indicate commits the
  local branch *lost* (squash, abandon, or similar).

If both yield "yes, remote clean + local missing
chids," the reset playbook above is the shortest
path. If only the first yields yes (no missing
chids, local just rewrote into broken state), you
can shrink step 1 of the playbook (work-loss risk
is gone) and just reset.

The fork point itself can be derived without
hand-scanning parent columns:
```
jj log -r 'heads(::<branch-A> & ::<branch-B>)'
```
Returns the latest commit that's an ancestor of
both — the divergence point.

## JJ-CONFLICT-README (the thing that shows in gitk)

When jj has a conflicted commit, it serializes the
conflict state into a git-side tree containing:

- `JJ-CONFLICT-README` — text file explaining the
  format.
- `.jjconflict-base-N/` — the base side(s) of the
  three-way merge.
- `.jjconflict-side-N/` — each conflict input.

Plain git tools (gitk, GitHub UI, etc.) display
this as real files. **Recoverable** — `jj` itself
knows the commit is conflicted and `jj resolve`
walks the inputs. Don't `git checkout` a conflicted
commit and start editing files directly; jj's
invariants get confused. The README itself includes
the recovery hint (`jj abandon` if you accidentally
end up in this state).

## Open questions

- **Merge direction.** Rebase `0.41.1` onto `0.42.0`
  → `0.43.0`-or-`0.42.1`, vs force-rewrite main.
  The first is the safe default; the second only if
  there's a positive reason to relitigate published
  history.
- **Conflict surface against 0.42.0.** Now that
  `init-clone-refactor` is clean, what's the actual
  diff vs current `main`? `0.42.0-1/2/3` rewrote
  `init.rs` / `scope.rs` / `common.rs`; `0.41.1-5/
  -6.x` also touched those. Real conflicts will
  surface only at the rebase step.

## References

- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign
  (0.41.1)` — full design captured in 0.42.0-4
  (docs-only commit on main).
- `notes/jj-revsets.md` — revset primitives used in
  the diagnostic and playbook above (`..` exclusive
  range, `::` inclusive DAG range, `heads()`,
  template `if(conflict, ...)`).
- `notes/substep-protocol.md` — close-out squash
  recipe for substep ladders. Note: this branch's
  `-6.0..6.5` ladder was authored before the
  protocol was formalized; the protocol postdates
  it.

# init-clone-refactor: recovery post-mortem + playbook

Drafted 2026-05-01 as a forward-looking brief while the
branch was still broken; rewritten 2026-05-02 after the
recovery completed. Documents what went wrong, the
playbook that fixed it, and the strategic decisions
still open.

## Status as of 2026-05-02 (post-recovery)

- `init-clone-refactor` bookmark is at `a8c1eefe`
  (chid `sumruqqomnzs`) — the published clean
  `0.41.1-6.5` commit.
- All 13 commits from fork point `ykxrlkkwqpyv`
  (`0.41.0`, cid `6747a27b`) through `0.41.1-6.5` are
  unconflicted. `cargo test --bins` passes 331/331 on
  the recovered tip.
- Recovery via `jj bookmark set init-clone-refactor
  -r init-clone-refactor@origin --allow-backwards
  -R .`. The published remote branch turned out to
  be the canonical-good version; local was holding a
  destructively-rewritten copy.
- 8 broken local-only chids still visible as orphans
  in `jj log` (no bookmark, GC-eligible). `jj abandon`
  cleanup is cosmetic, deferrable.

## Status as of 2026-05-01 (pre-recovery, historical)

- Branch existed locally with 8 commits all in
  conflict state: `0.41.1-0` (chore: open) → unnamed
  → `0.41.1-5` (init reshape) → `0.41.1-6.0..6.5`
  (POR baseline tests then progressive split of
  `create_local_repo`). Conflicts on the same files
  all the way down: `Cargo.toml`, `Cargo.lock`,
  `notes/todo.md`, `notes/chores-08.md` (3-sided),
  `src/config.rs`, `src/init.rs`, `src/clone.rs`.
- Original brief's hypothesis — "interrupted rebase
  or substep-style rewrite" — turned out to be wrong.
  See "Root cause" below.
- `@origin (ahead by at least 10 commits, behind by
  8 commits)` was what jj reported. The "ahead by
  *at least* 10" caveat (vs the precise "behind by
  8") was the tell that the remote held more commits
  than local — i.e. local had *lost* work, not just
  rewritten it. Worth noticing earlier next time.

## Why this matters (intent)

Refactor `init` and `clone` to share `init_one` /
`clone_one` primitives plus orchestrators
(`init_dual` / `clone_dual`). Eliminates duplicated
lifecycle logic between init and clone, and gives a
single chokepoint for `--scope`-related changes —
important because every subcommand picking up
`--scope` lands on the same `Scope` shape, and
reusable foundations mean those changes happen in
one place rather than parallel implementations.
Design captured in `notes/chores-07.md > ## init +
clone redesign (0.41.1)`.

## Root cause (post-mortem)

The bot thinks the local rewrites came from a
destructive `jj squash` (or equivalent operation)
that collapsed published commits `0.41.1-1`, `-2`,
`-3` into `-4`. Symptoms supporting this:

- Three chids existed on the remote (`mkwqykksykoo`
  `-1`, `plxsuynrtwks` `-2`, `xtrworrlkozn` `-3`)
  with no local counterpart at all.
- `uuzwzxzkszwq` (the local "unnamed" commit) shared
  the *same chid* as the remote `0.41.1-4` commit,
  but had its description stripped — consistent with
  a squash that pulled three commits' content into
  `-4`'s tree but lost the descriptions.
- Conflicts cascaded down `-5` and `-6.0..6.5`
  because those commits' parent tree no longer
  matched what they had been authored against — the
  rewritten `-4` diverged from what the descendants
  expected.

Net effect: 3 commits of real work disappeared from
local, `-4`'s description was lost, and every
descendant inherited a conflict against the changed
parent tree. The remote was untouched and held the
canonical-good version of all of it.

## Strategic decision (still deferred)

Keep `main` + the `0.42.0-X` series as-is. Now that
`init-clone-refactor` is recovered, we have a clean
branch from which to make the merge-direction call:

- rebase `0.41.1` on top of `0.42.0` as a `0.43.0`
  (or `0.42.1`), or
- vice versa (force-rewrite main — only if there's
  a reason that justifies rewriting published
  history),
- or some other topology.

Decision still deferred. The recovery removes the
"is the branch even salvageable" risk; the topology
question is now purely about scope and review
ergonomics, not technical feasibility.

## Recovery playbook (used 2026-05-02)

Reusable for similar "local rewrites broke a branch
that the remote holds in clean form" situations.
All read-only steps come first; mutation only after
verification.

1. **Back up first.** Cheap insurance.
   - `cp -a . ../<dir>-<date>-N` — full snapshot,
     includes `.jj/op` history. Or `rsync -a
     --exclude=target/ ./ ../<dir>/` if `target/`
     bloat matters.
   - `git clone <remote> ../<dir>-clone-<date>-N`
     — canonical published state, jj-free, browsable
     in gitk without local-rewrite confusion.
   - `jj op log -R . > ../<dir>-op-log-<date>-N.txt`
     — text-readable op log, independent of jj.

2. **Verify remote tip is unconflicted (read-only):**
   ```
   jj git fetch -R .
   jj log -R . -r '<fork-chid>::<branch>@origin' \
     -T '... if(conflict, "CONFLICT", "ok") ...'
   ```
   Every line must read `[ok]`. If any are
   `[CONFLICT]`, the remote isn't a safe target and
   this playbook doesn't apply.

3. **Verify local-only commits are all chid-rewrites
   of remote commits (read-only):**
   ```
   jj log -R . -r '<branch>@origin..<branch>' \
     -T 'change_id.short() ++ ...'
   ```
   Every chid here must also appear on the remote.
   If any chid is unique to local, the reset would
   lose work — investigate before proceeding.

4. **Reset bookmark to remote:**
   ```
   jj bookmark set <branch> -r <branch>@origin \
     --allow-backwards -R .
   ```
   The `--allow-backwards` is required because jj
   guards against accidental backwards moves.

5. **Sanity test:**
   ```
   jj new <branch> -R .
   cargo test --bins      # or full cargo clean ; cargo test for paranoia
   ```

6. **Optional cleanup:** `jj abandon` the orphan
   local-only chids if you don't want them in
   `jj log`. Cosmetic; jj's GC eventually prunes
   them anyway.

## Diagnostic technique (reusable)

Two questions to ask when a branch is mysteriously
conflicted:

- **Are the same chids on the remote in clean form?**
  `jj log -r '<fork>::<branch>@origin'` with the
  conflict-status template tells you whether the
  remote has a working version of every commit.
- **Is local missing any chids the remote has?**
  Compare chid lists between
  `<fork>::<branch>@origin` and
  `<fork>::<branch>` — gaps indicate commits the
  local branch *lost* (squash, abandon, or similar).

If both yield "yes, remote clean + local missing
chids," the reset playbook above is the shortest
path. If only the first yields yes (no missing
chids, local just rewrote into broken state), you
can shrink step 1 of the playbook (work-loss risk
is gone) and just reset.

The fork point itself can be derived without
hand-scanning parent columns:
```
jj log -r 'heads(::<branch-A> & ::<branch-B>)'
```
Returns the latest commit that's an ancestor of
both — the divergence point.

## JJ-CONFLICT-README (the thing that shows in gitk)

When jj has a conflicted commit, it serializes the
conflict state into a git-side tree containing:

- `JJ-CONFLICT-README` — text file explaining the
  format.
- `.jjconflict-base-N/` — the base side(s) of the
  three-way merge.
- `.jjconflict-side-N/` — each conflict input.

Plain git tools (gitk, GitHub UI, etc.) display
this as real files. **Recoverable** — `jj` itself
knows the commit is conflicted and `jj resolve`
walks the inputs. Don't `git checkout` a conflicted
commit and start editing files directly; jj's
invariants get confused. The README itself includes
the recovery hint (`jj abandon` if you accidentally
end up in this state).

## Open questions

- **Merge direction.** Rebase `0.41.1` onto `0.42.0`
  → `0.43.0`-or-`0.42.1`, vs force-rewrite main.
  The first is the safe default; the second only if
  there's a positive reason to relitigate published
  history.
- **Conflict surface against 0.42.0.** Now that
  `init-clone-refactor` is clean, what's the actual
  diff vs current `main`? `0.42.0-1/2/3` rewrote
  `init.rs` / `scope.rs` / `common.rs`; `0.41.1-5/
  -6.x` also touched those. Real conflicts will
  surface only at the rebase step.

## References

- `notes/chores-07.md > ## init + clone redesign
  (0.41.1)` — full design captured in 0.42.0-4
  (docs-only commit on main).
- `notes/jj-revsets.md` — revset primitives used in
  the diagnostic and playbook above (`..` exclusive
  range, `::` inclusive DAG range, `heads()`,
  template `if(conflict, ...)`).
- `notes/substep-protocol.md` — close-out squash
  recipe for substep ladders. Note: this branch's
  `-6.0..6.5` ladder was authored before the
  protocol was formalized; the protocol postdates
  it.
